title: PRINCIPLES AND GUIDELINES FOR THE EXCHANGE OF INFORMATION BETWEEN IMPORTING AND EXPORTING COUNTRIES TO SUPPORT THE TRADE IN FOOD 1 CAC/GL 89-2016

sections.section: PRINCIPLES AND GUIDELINES FOR THE EXCHANGE OF INFORMATION BETWEEN IMPORTING AND EXPORTING COUNTRIES TO SUPPORT THE TRADE IN FOOD 1 CAC/GL 89-2016
sections.summary: Document title and adoption note: CAC/GL 89‑2016 adopted in 2016; guidelines also apply to feed for food‑producing animals when this could impact food safety or fair practices in food trade.

Explains that while most food trade occurs without NFCS information exchange, importing countries may require exchange when risks are high or assurances cannot be achieved otherwise, and it references relevant Codex texts to guide assessments.

States the objectives to guide competent authorities on when exchange of information is necessary for assessing relevant components of an exporting country's NFCS and to simplify and harmonize the information and process of exchange.

Defines the scope as addressing situations where competent authorities may need to exchange information to assess relevant components of an exporting country's NFCS covering a product or group of products prior to initiation or maintenance of trade.

Information exchange and assessment are justified when the risk from a specific food or group requires evaluation of whether the relevant component(s) of the exporting country's National Food Control System (NFCS) appropriately manage those risks, and importing and exporting competent authorities should focus on those relevant components, avoid imposing unjustified higher standards or routine re‑assessments, recognize different system designs and existing official controls, and limit exchanged information to what is essential and related to the exported food.

Exchanges must be conducted between the relevant competent authorities and be transparent, structured, focused, interactive and timely; use English or an agreed language, permit electronic transmission and referencing of existing information, recognize prior experience and confidence from other assessments, and avoid requesting commercially sensitive business‑specific information unless essential for public health and protected from misuse.

Where exchanges are needed, the importing country should clearly specify required information, rationale, methods and timelines, review existing information, provide a description of its NFCS objectives and performance to help exporters respond, focus requests on whether the exporting country's relevant NFCS components achieve required outcomes, engage promptly to close gaps and limit update requests to changed elements; the exporting country should describe its relevant NFCS per Codex guidance, engage promptly and notify the importing country of relevant changes.

Exporting countries may prepare standardised responses describing relevant NFCS components (e.g., legal framework; competent authority capability, roles, independence and procedures; official controls, verification, enforcement; laboratory capacity; emergency response and recall; training; monitoring; and criteria for approving food business operators), and importing countries should be flexible about format and only request additional information to address gaps or unaddressed risks.
